The Neoclouds have started running ($NBIS $IREN $CRWV)

I think cooling is "just warming up".

text:
PO, CPO를 포함한 Photonics 섹터에서
PIC 설계, 제조사들은 200G/lane이상을 이야기하며 그 이상을 바라보지만

제대로된 EIC 200G/lane을 설계하고
그걸 Foundry로 만들 수 있는 기업은 한정적인데 사람들은 그걸 간과하고 있는듯하다.

Photonics는 PIC, EIC, DSP, LASER, PD Detector, 차세대 패키징이 한번에 가능해야 비로소 제대로 만들어졌다 할 수 있다.

PIC 200G를 시연에 성공했어도, PD Detector가 그걸 못받아 들이는 사양이면 무용지물이고, EIC가 신호를 제대로 못주면 그것 또한 의미 없는 것이다.

그 신호를 밀어줄 EIC/driver, 받아줄 PD/TIA, 보정할 DSP, 버틸 laser, 그리고 그걸 낮은 loss와 높은 yield로 묶는 패키징이 같이 맞아야 한다.

결국 Photonics는 부품 기술이 아니라, 시스템 통합 싸움이다.

text:
$AMD just reported a monster ER and sent every CPU stock to the moon. Up +15% after hours.

Since I started publicly sharing my portfolio I’ve been clear about where I’m putting my money and why:

The AI buildout: 

Photonics / Memory / CPUs / NeoClouds

So why CPUs and why now?

$AMD said it best on the call: in the past the CPU-to-GPU ratio was 1-to-4 or 1-to-8. Now it’s moving toward 1-to-1. And with agentic AI, you could end up with more CPUs than GPUs. That’s a structural shift.

Here’s how my CPU basket is doing since I entered:

$INTC (January) – entry at ~$55, bought the local top because I’m the worst trader ever. Now +105%.

$AMD (late March) – entry at ~$202, bought the local bottom because I’m the best trader ever. Now +76%.

$ARM (late March) –entry at $137 after the CPU for AI thesis started gaining traction. Now +62%.

If you think you’re late I can assure you you’re not. Not super early either. But agentic 

AI spending is just getting started and CPUs are in the critical path of every dollar spent.

Still bullish all CPU plays.

In a new blog, Joseph Chon explains why integrating MACsec directly into coherent DSPs inside ZR/ZR+ pluggable modules represents a meaningful shift in how scale-across networks are secured. Compared to embedding MACsec in switch ASICs or placing dedicated security devices on circuit boards, module-based MACsec reduces cost, complexity and deployment risk while making upgrades significantly easier. Marvell has already introduced several industry firsts enabling this approach, including the Electra 2nm 1.6T coherent DSP, the Libra 800G coherent DSP and the Aquila M coherent-lite DSP, all with integrated MACsec. By 2030, pluggables are projected to account for approximately 85% of scale-across ports shipped. text:
Threadguy explains why he slammed Zcash this weekend 

“Everybody in the stock market is so rich right now. Everybody is up so much money… there is just trillions of dollars that have been printed in the stock market”

“They’re ripping everything. We’re doing photonics now, everything’s an AI bottleneck… anything that could be remotely related to AI that hasn’t pumped, rip that, South Korea stocks now… rip that”

“When I saw the metals to full AI to South Korean stock rotation I’m like people are desperate for some reflexivity”

“There’s three assets in crypto that just haven’t moved Hyperliquid, Zcash and Bitcoin. Zcash is like an $8B coin… it’s worth nothing”

“I’m watching Google add $500B in a day and I’m saying to myself man they’re gonna pop it”

“Bitcoin, Zcash and Hyperliquid all of a sudden look good. I was bidding Zcash a little bit but then I just slammed over the weekend”

“Bitcoin, Zcash, Hyperliquid are the three set up… I just got the tingle”

Optimistically you’re looking at 40-60% CAGR on photonics through 2028 with 

CPO as the accelerant. Memory, specifically HBM, is already running 50%+ growth and that probably holds or steepens as cluster density increases.

The Goldman re-rate just gave institutional cover for what was already obvious to anyone close to the hardware stack.

text:
La @utec_tgo continúa fortaleciendo su presencia internacional en el ámbito científico y tecnológico con el nombramiento de la Dra. Ana Karen Reyes como Vicepresidenta Asociada de Vinculación Global con Miembros de la IEEE Photonics Society para el periodo 2026–2028, uno de los organismos más importantes a nivel mundial en investigación y desarrollo tecnológico. 🌎🔬

Este reconocimiento refleja la calidad académica y de investigación que impulsa la UTEC, fortaleciendo la generación de conocimiento, la innovación y la vinculación con comunidades científicas internacionales, en concordancia con la visión educativa promovida por el gobernador @juliomenchaca_ y el secretario de Educación Pública de Hidalgo, @natycastrejonv 📚✨

text:
If you invest in photonics, please read this.

I spent this weekend researching the OCS supply chain.

Quick lesson:

> OCS = Optical Circuit Switching
> Hyperscalers want lower power, lower latency, and faster AI networks
> OCS helps by keeping light as light
> Instead of optical → electrical → optical conversion at every hop, the signal can stay optical through the switch

That is why this is becoming a real theme.

$LITE already has a multi-year, multi-billi on-dollar OCS purchase agreement.

Management called OCS the “biggest single tightrope” the company is walking because customers want output faster than the supply chain can ramp.

$COHR doubled its OCS market opportunity estimate to over $4B.

They also said they fixed a production bottleneck and are now ramping output across two facilities.

$HUBN has Polatis, one of the more hidden OCS assets in public markets.

They secured major hyperscale OCS orders and opened a new Poland facility to increase capacity at least 5x over two years.

This is still early.

There is also an entire supply chain here.

So I built a public OCS map with 12 companies worth knowing.

I currently own 4 of them.

I want to own 2 more.

Read it here:

https://t.co/97bLrkIvE8

text:
$IPGP IPG Photonics: The Strategic Pivot, Path to Recovery, and Investment Thesis.

IPG Photonics is the dominant player in fiber lasers, but dominance in industrial cutting has become a more complicated position to hold. Chinese competitors have driven sustained price pressure in that segment, and the margin profile that once defined IPG's quality premium has eroded as a result. The strategic response — pivoting toward medical technology, battery welding, and defense — is the right directional move, but it's an early-stage reorientation against a backdrop of near-term financial pressure.

Recent results show demand recovering and revenue growing, which confirms that the core business remains viable. The more important question is whether that top-line recovery translates into margin restoration, and the answer so far is incomplete. Free cash flow conversion has been inconsistent, and profitability hasn't yet reflected the volume improvement in a way that validates the thesis.

Leadership has been overhauled. The new CEO is focused on operational discipline and broadening the product portfolio toward integrated laser systems — a higher-value configuration that moves IPG up the stack from component supplier to solutions provider. That's the right ambition, but integrated systems require different sales motions, longer customer relationships, and more complex delivery capabilities than standalone laser units. The organizational transition to support that shift is still underway.

The valuation is carrying a recovery premium that the financials haven't yet earned. The stock is pricing in a successful execution of the strategic pivot, which means margin expansion and free cash flow improvement need to materialize on a visible timeline to justify current levels. A high-quality business with a credible long-term direction, but the entry point demands that the complex pieces — new leadership, new markets, new product configurations — come together with reasonable speed.

The upside is real if the pivot lands. Medical, battery, and defense are structurally better markets than commodity industrial cutting, and IPG's technical foundation is strong enough to compete credibly in all three. The risk is execution timeline — the gap between strategic intent and financial proof points is still wide, and the stock doesn't offer much patience buffer at current multiples.

Combined value: ~8 billion SEK. https://t.co/k9EMCinhs5 It is framed as these investors are driving this on speculation. Yes maybe, that is of course possible. Or is there another angle? My take, SEK8B..what? Hence my reaction would probably be the opposite, this could be worth investigating: What thesis are these allocators actually trading? Is it pure speculation with SEK 8B in play? Who sits on these SEK8B? EFN however have asked "Richard Schatz, senior researcher in photonics at RISE Research Institutes of Sweden and former consultant for Sivers", for his opinion about Sivers. So what did Mr. Richard Schatz say: "There are several companies that manufacture these kinds of lasers, and Sivers is far from alone. I don't understand why the company has become so hyped. There are many players, not just in Europe and the US, but also in China," says Schatz. Honestly I have been COO and CEO at Sivers for almost 10 years 2015-2024 (2015 we had 18 headcount I known everyone in the Company until 2024) and also ask Board members that was in the board many years before me, both for Sivers and former CST Global (Sivers Photonics today) and none of them can recollect Mr. Schatz. I also sent an email, asking the reporter that interviewed Mr S and asked when he claims to have been a consultant at Sivers. Without reply. This doesn't really matter, it is just shows where the digging stops. Sivers Semiconductors is one of a handful of merchant InP laser suppliers globally. They have disclosed partnerships with POET Technologies, Jabil (1.6T LRO), O-Net, Ayar Labs and operate in the External Light Source category that scale-up CPO architectures depend on. Either are all these US investors dramatically wrong about the photonics layer of AI infrastructure, or the institutional allocators routing through US brokerages have identified what most Swedish investors haven't yet. Maybe this is worth investigating which? To be 100% clear no one knows (not even me), who has the right hypothesis, that we might know in 2028-2030 time frame. To help anyone intressted, I made this free and open substack to explain how the money flows. As they said in Washington follow the money... text:
This is something I didn't discuss back then, but it is essential knowledge

To understand $SIVE, we must recognize that two distinct branches exist:

-InP Lasers for Photonics (CPO Ramp up)

This is the segment currently catapulting the stock price

-MmWave Technology (Wireless)

In this branch, $SIVE possesses Radio Frequency (RF) technology capable of transmitting data over the air at ultra high speeds over short distances

Essentially, they manufacture chips and antennas for 5G, 6G, and Satellites (Satcom)

This is where the partnership with Tachyon Networks comes in developing 60GHz transceivers for $1.5M

Following a previous $3M deal for 28GHz transceivers

These technologies have reached a critical milestone

They are in the mass production phase with recurring orders

This is vital because while technologies like those from $ALMU or $KOPN are very promising, they are still far from this specific phase

The phase that often becomes a bottleneck and provides true vertical integration into the market

$SIVE’s wireless branch, however, is mature

While this second branch might seem less "flashy", it carries significant weight

Regarding the current revaluation of $SIVE, it is crucial as these figures are reflected in the earnings reports

Poor performance in this segment would tarnish the InP Laser branch, which is what the market cares about most right now

Furthermore, the fact that they are already operating at 28GHz and 60GHz proves that their silicon (InP) is reliable under real world conditions

Something that Photonics (CPO) customers examine under a magnifying glass before signing any contracts

@UCF Thank you for holding the graduation ceremony last night, 5/9/26.  
I am so very proud of Joshua’s accomplishments on working hard to attain a Bachelor of Science degree in the field of Optics and Photonics.  He is a bright young man.  
Good luck in Delaware with the your next chapter of life.
I love you, Mom. 
#JoshuaLHendry

user_name: ゆるっと半導体｜AI・半導体ニュースを毎朝やさしく解説
text:
『Scintil Photonicsって、何してる会社？』 光集積回路（光信号を処理するチップ）の設計スタートアップなんだ。CEOのMatt Crowleyは物理学者出身で、研究室の技術を実際のものづくりに変える専門家。

2026年の今、Coherent・Lumentumといった光トランシーバー大手がCPO（共栄接光学）チップの増産に追われてるんだけど、こういう新興企業が『次の世代の回路設計』で割り込むパターンが増えてるんだ。

NVIDIAのBlackwell Ultra向け光学接続ケーブルの需要が膨らむなか、既存メーカーだけでは賄えない領域が出てきてるんだよ。Matt Crowleyのような『研究から量産への翻訳人』が活躍する土壌ができてる、ってのが今の市場の面白さなんだ。

→ AI時代の半導体投資では、有名大手より『こっそり成長する周辺企業』を先に見つけた人が勝つんだ。

text:
European and Asian Firms Express Strong Interest in Stakes in Canada’s Photonics Fabrication Centre Spin-Out 

The Government of Canada, through Industry Minister Joly and the National Research Council of Canada (NRC), is advancing the spin-out of the Canadian Photonics Fabrication Centre (CPFC)—North America’s only end-to-end pure-play compound semiconductor wafer fabrication facility—into a commercially oriented entity. The move, first signaled in Budget 2025 and formally announced in early May 2026, aims to attract private-sector capital to scale operations, expand Canada’s photonic semiconductor supply chain, and accelerate innovation in AI data centres, quantum computing, fibre-optic networks, and advanced technologies.

Key Context and Objectives

Strategic repositioning: Transition the publicly owned CPFC from a research-focused facility to a scaled commercial operation while maintaining majority Canadian ownership and control. The government will retain a stake, ensuring the entity remains anchored in Canada and continues to create high-skilled jobs.

Investment attraction: A formal call for proposals has been issued to secure private investment from “trusted partners.” The goal is to enhance domestic manufacturing capacity, support Canadian SMEs, and strengthen supply-chain resilience in critical photonics technologies.

Global interest: Minister Joly confirmed that firms from Europe and Asia have already expressed keen interest in taking stakes or investing in the facility. She highlighted opportunities to build a “middle-power alliance” by deepening ties with these partners, complementing North American demand.

Impact

This initiative positions Canada as a leader in photonic semiconductors—a foundational technology for AI, quantum, and high-performance computing—at a time of global supply-chain pressures. By leveraging international capital while safeguarding Canadian sovereignty, the spin-out is expected to drive economic growth, productivity, and technological sovereignty. Canadian companies such as Ranovus have already signaled intent to participate as investors or operators.

Firms Express Strong Interest in Stakes in Canada’s Photonics Fabrication Centre Spin-Out The Government of Canada, through Industry Minister Joly and the National Research Council of Canada (NRC), is advancing the spin-out of the Canadian Photonics Fabrication Centre (CPFC)—North America’s only end-to-end pure-play compound semiconductor wafer fabrication facility—into a commercially oriented entity. The move, first signaled in Budget 2025 and formally announced in early May 2026, aims to attract private-sector capital to scale operations, expand Canada’s photonic semiconductor supply chain, and accelerate innovation in AI data centres, quantum computing, fibre-optic networks, and advanced technologies. Key Context and Objectives Strategic repositioning: Transition the publicly owned CPFC from a research-focused facility to a scaled commercial operation while maintaining majority Canadian ownership and control. The government will retain a stake, ensuring the entity remains anchored in Canada and continues to create high-skilled jobs. Investment attraction: A formal call for proposals has been issued to secure private investment from “trusted partners.” The goal is to enhance domestic manufacturing capacity, support Canadian SMEs, and strengthen supply-chain resilience in critical photonics technologies. Global interest: Minister Joly confirmed that firms from Europe and Asia have already expressed keen interest in taking stakes or investing in the facility. She highlighted opportunities to build a “middle-power alliance” by deepening ties with these partners, complementing North American demand. Impact This initiative positions Canada as a leader in photonic semiconductors—a foundational technology for AI, quantum, and high-performance computing—at a time of global supply-chain pressures. By leveraging international capital while safeguarding Canadian sovereignty, the spin-out is expected to drive economic growth, productivity, and technological sovereignty. Canadian companies such as Ranovus have already signaled intent to participate as investors or operators."}}}, "rest_id": "2053583530693914977", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "28", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Everything looks to be going to plan. Another situation with these photonics stocks where listening to management talk is way more important than the numbers. This is a forward growth story about becoming "a top 3 global laser supplier" with the ramp not starting until H2. The company raised full year guidance to $1.1B from $1B. Talked about their 900,00 sq. ft in U.S. factory space doing up to 30% production in USA on top of their 700K+ Taiwanese space Discussed their CPO laser which they plan to 80x production of (said they're in talks with several customers on this) Said margins could get to 40% towards end of year up from ~30% Working with 3 hyperscalers, one qualified, another almost qualified. Lin said real demand is $1.4-$1.5B even though they can only fulfill $1.1B. The company re-affirmed their target of hitting $450 monthly revenue in June/July 2027 which would imply an annual revenue run rate of $5B+ at a current market cap of ~$12B. This is what has triggered the massive re-rate. Execution risk remains crazy high but management just doubled down on what they previously said which is why the stock trade as high as $190 recently. I just don't think the strategically important infrastructure of AAOI (domestic laser fabs, vertical integration, CPO laser) is being given enough credit here. This could easily still be a multi-bagger if they execute, with the possibility of being worth several tens of billions in the next few years hence why it's the largest position in my photonics fund."}}}, "rest_id": "2052509803302555865", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "132071", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2052528433226526947", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "85", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
COHR may be one of the most important AI infrastructure companies most investors still don’t fully understand.

Not GPUs. Not ASICs. Not memory.

Photonics.

Coherent sits at the physical layer of the AI buildout — the optical spine connecting GPUs, switches, and data centers.

Q3 FY26 revenue hit a record $1.81B, datacenter revenue grew 37% YoY, and NVIDIA just wrote a $2B check to secure the roadmap.

The bull case: 6-inch InP fabs, 800G → 1.6T → CPO, and a $23B growth SAM.

The bear case: debt, valuation, and execution risk.

Full deep dive below.

"to_index": 3582}, {"from_index": 3651, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 3685}]}, "text": "When you follow $SIVE closely over time, one thing that stands out is how many parallel tracks the company is advancing simultaneously. It rarely feels like a story built around a single product or market. Instead, it is a platform company where the same core photonics technology is applied across large and growing domains. One update that highlighted this breadth came on March 11, 2026, when $SIVE announced that a strategic LiDAR customer had chosen to integrate its photonics technology across all platforms. Production ramp is scheduled to begin in Q4 2026, targeting automotive and industrial applications. This is not a limited component agreement. $SIVE is supplying high-power continuous wave DFB laser diode arrays together with optical amplifiers integrated throughout the customer’s portfolio. Estimated cumulative lifetime revenue potential is $53–138 million, based on known volumes today, with clear upside as the customer scales. All indications point to this customer being $AEVA. Their 4D FMCW LiDAR technology, used in Atlas and X1, is engineered for long range, precise velocity measurement, and high resolution — capabilities required for real-world autonomous systems where safety and reliability are critical. $SIVE’s lasers help enable that performance, and their implementation across all platforms demonstrates the strength of $SIVE’s III-V photonics platform. It is worth stepping back and reflecting on what this means. The same technology stack powering highly efficient 1.6T transceivers with Jabil for AI data center infrastructure is now also finding its way into LiDAR sensors for trucks, passenger vehicles, robotics, and industrial automation. This ability to leverage one core competence across end-markets is one of the most compelling aspects of the $SIVE investment case. It lowers development risk, creates multiple growth engines, and allows ramps to mature at their own pace. $AEVA already has notable commercial momentum. They are integrated into $NVDA DRIVE Hyperion with volume production targeted for 2028 and have a partnership with Daimler Truck. As more OEMs, fleet operators, and robotics companies adopt similar LiDAR solutions, each new design win or deployment can drive higher shipment volumes. Automotive platforms often run for 8–10 years or longer, while industrial and robotics applications can create more stable long-term revenue streams. That gives $SIVE substantial room to grow beyond today’s base-case estimate. In the current market discussion, much focus is understandably on nearer-term opportunities — FWA and mmWave with Tachyon, or SATCOM progress through https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp and $YSS. Those areas are important and may generate revenue momentum earlier. But the LiDAR ramp is a meaningful and underappreciated building block for 2027 and beyond. It does not begin as a small proof-of-concept. It starts with a strategic customer embedding $SIVE technology deeply from the beginning. This kind of multi-domain execution is what gives $SIVE its resilience and long-term potential. Photonics is the common thread connecting AI infrastructure (CPO), wireless connectivity (mmWave/FWA), satellite communications, and autonomous perception systems (LiDAR). With a reported pipeline exceeding $453 million and several parallel ramps in different stages of maturity, the company is not overly dependent on any single market or customer. The LiDAR opportunity still feels underappreciated. It may not dominate the daily discussion, but it starts contributing real production volumes already in Q4 this year. And as $AEVA expands in automotive and industrial markets, $SIVE’s participation across all platforms positions the company to capture a growing share of that opportunity. It is a reminder of why the longer-term thesis around $SIVE remains compelling: a specialized photonics capability that can scale across multiple high-growth verticals, creating revenue streams with significant long-term compounding potential."}}}, "rest_id": "2052353159398392187", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "524828", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2052515063945199683", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3227", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

PC에서 파워가 전력을 만들고, 메인보드·그래픽카드·RAM이 그 전력을 나눠 쓰듯이, 포토닉스 링크에서는 Laser가 만든 Optical Power가 Coupler → Waveguide → Modulator → Waveguide → PD로 이동한다. 문제는 이 과정에서 계속 Loss가 발생한다는 것이다. 보통 Laser Power는 mW가 아니라 dBm으로 본다. 쉽게 말하면, 100mW = 20 dBm 400mW = 26 dBm 이다. 400mW Laser는 100mW보다 출력은 4배 강하지만, dBm 기준으로는 +6 dB 높은 Laser다. 예를 들어 Laser 출력이 10 dBm이라고 해보자. Coupler에서 2 dB, Waveguide에서 2 dB, Modulator에서 3 dB 손실이 나면, PD에 도착하는 Optical Power는 결국 3 dBm 수준이다. 즉, 앞에서 아무리 강한 Laser를 넣어도 중간 Loss가 크면 PD가 받아들이는 Power는 크게 줄어든다. 반대로 말하면, Laser 자체가 무조건 강해야 좋은 시스템이 되는 것도 아니다. Laser에서 PD까지 가는 경로의 Loss가 낮으면, 더 낮은 Laser Power로도 충분히 쓸 수 있다. 그래서 포토닉스에서 중요한 질문은 단순히 “Laser가 얼마나 세냐?”가 아니다. 진짜 핵심은 이거다. 그 Laser Power를 PD까지 얼마나 덜 잃고 전달하느냐. 결국 Photonics는 Laser 싸움이면서 동시에 dB Loss 싸움이다."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2051926679506088437"}}, "rest_id": "2052017338346885248", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "48311", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2052513391898739130", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "768", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Anyone else grab $AAOI in the 130's?

Everything looks to be going to plan.

Another situation with these photonics stocks where listening to management talk is way more important than the numbers.

This is a forward growth story about becoming "a top 3 global laser supplier" with the ramp not starting until H2.

The company raised full year guidance to $1.1B from $1B.
Talked about their 900,00 sq. ft in U.S. factory space doing up to 30% production in USA on top of their 700K+ Taiwanese space
Discussed their CPO laser which they plan to 80x production of (said they're in talks with several customers on this)
Said margins could get to 40% towards end of year up from ~30%
Working with 3 hyperscalers, one qualified, another almost qualified.
Lin said real demand is $1.4-$1.5B even though they can only fulfill $1.1B.

The company re-affirmed their target of hitting $450 monthly revenue in June/July 2027 which would imply an annual revenue run rate of $5B+ at a current market cap of ~$12B. This is what has triggered the massive re-rate.

Execution risk remains crazy high but management just doubled down on what they previously said which is why the stock trade as high as $190 recently. 

I just don't think the strategically important infrastructure of AAOI (domestic laser fabs, vertical integration, CPO laser) is being given enough credit here. 

This could easily still be a multi-bagger if they execute, with the possibility of being worth several tens of billions in the next few years hence why it's the largest position in my photonics fund.

text:
My exposure to photonics 

*You've basically called all of them

Think these are positioned well to capture capex ramp over the next 3-5 years 

SIVE 
- Sivers Semiconductors (lasers for CPO optical engines)

OE Solutions  
- InP lasers + ELSFP modules for CPO

LPK (LPKF)    
- Laser systems for glass interposers & advanced photonics packaging

ALMU          
- Aeluma (compound semiconductor photonics / quantum dot lasers)

Episil Precision (https://t.co/TuTwLjmIHE) 
- Silicon photonics epi wafers

IQE 
- Compound semi epi wafers (InP/GaAs/GaN) for photonics

SOI (Soitec)  
- Engineered substrates (SOI) critical for silicon photonics

ShunShin (https://t.co/f1ZwK4ljHn) 
- Optical packaging & assembly for CPO

text:
$inv
Reposting again 

Everyone still has eyes on the memory and photonics  trade $SNDK $MU $DRAM $LITE $COHR $SIVE

Whilst there might be some more room in these still, they are becoming somewhat crowded. 

Exactly why Goldman is saying liquid cooling is the next trade like memory. 

Liquid cooling has lagged the overall data centre trade.

All the private cooling companies are getting hoovered up at the moment in big private deals. $ETN and Boyd. $ECL and Cool IT. $VRT and Strategic Thermal Labs. 
These big companies are trying to build out their end to end solutions for next gen cooling. 

Then you’ve got the Series B round of $jci in @AccelsiusATX $inv

It’s actually hard to get exposure to a public pure play cooling company right now. 
I think $inv is the closest way with there 43.2% holding in @AccelsiusATX 

From my research it’s abundantly clear the direction liquid cooling is going.

$inv
From the GS global equites call this AM

They expect liquid cooling to be the next bottle neck. Similar to memory last year $mu $sndk $5930.sk SK Hynix

A great example of this was: the memory theme that emerged late last year. The current bottlenecks now exist in fiber https://t.co/B6Ws7wS5lD

text:
Another great day for semiconductor, photonics, space and energy sectors today.  Overall, AI infrastructure continues to be the strongest theme in this market right now.  
Not everything is working — this is a selective market — but quality names tied to compute, memory, optical networking, power, cooling and space infrastructure are seeing strong money rotation and momentum.  The market keeps rewarding the right sectors and patience.

text:
Lumentum $LITE joining the Nasdaq 100 ($NDX) is a massive signal for the photonics space.

Stock is up 16% in a single day.

The inclusion brings passive flows from funds.

It’s a huge validation for the optical hardware space which brings more inflows into other photonics names such as $AAOI and $SIVE, which are up 24% and 13% respectively.

It feels like institutions are finally starting to realize how critical this part of the supply chain is.

TLDR: $MTSI – the analog name inside every 800G/1.6T optical module Rubin needs.

Silicon Photonics & CPO (#2 this week) (co-packaged optics, lasers built into the chip) (#2 this week) is the bottleneck.

The volume player in the basket: $MTSI.

Drivers, TIAs, lasers.

They sit where the modules can't ship without them.

text:
the scale up shift to CPO/NPO/LPO seems more quickly than we expected.

the truth is MAC layer is ready, both NVLINK( and UALink(ETH) are ready for copper/optical PHY in protocol level: they are just waiting for the photonics industry to catch up.

CPO is fully verified on both NVDA switch and AVGO switch with TSMC.

Chinese CSP/GPU:
1. ALI: full optical LPO.
2. Huawei: full optical LPO.

US:
1. Google: full optical LPO + OCS
2. Nvidia: NVLINK mixed copper / optical ,  NVL72 scale-up(copper) ,  8 x NVL72 intra connection(CPO) = NVL576,  scale-out: optical.
full CPO in 2028 on Feyman(NVL1152 full CPO).

the investment to $LITE and $COHR  and $GLW shows NVIDIA is trying intensify the relation and production on optical, and shift to optical. just like one year ago Jensen visited Korea to secure the HBM.

the optical usage is 30X increase than traditional server. and the industry is not ready... as NVIDIA's development is much more fast than the photonics industry.

text:
Like many other FinX folks, I took a small position in $SIVE. This is one of my favorite speculative plays at the moment at ~3% of my portfolio, but….

I am keeping my eye on how well the 130 person org can juggle 5G mmWave + AI-DC photonics + LIDAR + SATCOM beamformers + defense + Nasdaq dual-listing prep + audit uplift. 

They have a lot of moving parts at the moment for a relatively small team ($LITE has 10k+ headcount for context). Perfect execution is imperative to maintain the 3B+ MC thesis. 

Right now, I still love this play at <50SEK

text:
The technical setup for POET Technologies $POET  at $10.95 reflects a stock that is currently one of the most explosive "AI-Photonics" plays in the market. Following a multi-year basing period, POET has entered a high-conviction Strong Bullish Alignment, recently surging on massive institutional volume.

Here is the strategic breakdown of the "Optical Interposer" pivot and the technical setup for the week ahead.

📊 Technical Health Check: The "Breakaway" Gap
The system is flashing Bullish Alignment, with the price now trading in a "Power Trend" above both the Weekly Kumo and the Daily EMA.

Weekly Macro Trend: POET has successfully cleared the "Cloud of Death" that capped its price throughout 2024–2025. The Weekly Kijun-sen (purple) at $9.60 has officially flipped from resistance to a primary floor. As long as the stock stays above $9.50 on a weekly close, the macro bull case is the dominant narrative.
The Volume Profile: On May 8, POET surged 14.3% on over 70 million shares—more than triple its average daily volume. In technical analysis, this "Volume Spike + Price Breakout" is the ultimate confirmation that institutional accumulation is occurring.
The Signal Success: The Long Signal from April 20 at $8.59 is now up +27.5%. This trade was a "Macro Match" from day one, proving that the system correctly identified the shift in AI data center demand toward optical interconnects.

🧬 Fundamental Catalyst: 800G Scale-Up & Q1 Earnings

The $10.95 price point reflects the market's realization that POET is transitioning from a "research lab" to a "high-volume manufacturer."

Q1 Earnings (Thursday, May 14, 2026): POET is scheduled to report after the close this Thursday. While analysts expect a loss of $0.04/share, the market is solely focused on the Revenue Guidance and Backlog.

The $5M+ "Infinity" Order: Earlier this year, POET received a production order exceeding $5M for its 800G Infinity optical engines. Management has guided for delivery in H2 2026, and investors want to see if this has expanded.
The 1.6T Partnership (LITEON/Lessengers): POET is currently co-developing 1.6T and 3.2T optical modules. Any update on prototypes for these next-generation AI speeds (expected late 2026) would likely send the stock back toward its 52-week high of $15.50.
Manufacturing Readiness: High-volume production in Malaysia is slated to begin in Q2 (now). Any confirmation that the facility is "live" and shipping light-source products is the primary "Go/No-Go" signal for the bulls.

💡 Hybrid System Conclusion
The Bottom Line:  $POET is the "AI Bottleneck Buster." >The technicals are in "Strong Bullish" alignment because POET's technology addresses the #1 problem in AI data centers: Heat and Latency. 
The 70M volume day is a definitive market "vote" that this tech is real.
Trading Rule: With a massive 27% gain already in the bag, the risk of a "sell the news" event on Thursday is high. Do not chase the $11.00 level. Instead, look for a "Buy the Dip" opportunity if the stock pullbacks to the $9.50–$9.80 support zone (the Daily EMA). As long as it holds above $9.50, the trend is up.
Final Thought: We follow the photons, not the hype. POET has $430M in cash to bridge the gap to 2027 profitability.  Manufacturing scale-up is the current reality for $POET.

Summary: Resistance is at $12.30; Support is at $9.60. Thursday's call will determine if POET is a "Niche Component Maker" or the "Standard Interconnect" for the next generation of AI clusters.

CEO Vickram Vathulya previously led Nuvotronics through a strategic and operational transformation focused on long-term growth and value creation. Todd Thomson adds major financial and strategic firepower from Citigroup, GE Capital and Bain & Co. MD Wireless Harish Krishnaswamy brings world-class mmWave expertise and experience around strategic semiconductor funding opportunities like the CHIPS Act. CFO Heine Thorsgaard has spent 20+ years driving growth, operational efficiency and long-term value creation across technology companies, most recently as CFO of Napatech. And CRO Raymond Biagan has a strong track record of driving major revenue growth and customer expansion across the semiconductor industry, including at $GFS. text:
10. What is the market still missing?

In my opinion, the market is still looking too much at what $SIVE is today and not enough at what could happen if several ramps start layering in at the same time.

No one knows exactly how photonics, pluggables and CPO will develop, but I believe it will shock a lot of people how big it ultimately becomes. The market has a hard time grasping the scale before everything really kicks off. But once it does, the company could already be valued at multiples far beyond where we are today.

Jabil validation, WIN scaling, pipeline growth, institutional interest and Nasdaq path are lining up.

But if even a few of these ramps hit, I think the market may have to rethink what kind of company $SIVE is becoming.

$SIVE has been grinding for years. Now everything is starting to line up at once.

To me, this no longer looks like a small speculative semiconductor story. It looks like a company that may be entering the steep part of the curve just as several massive infrastructure markets begin accelerating at the same time.

And I still think the market is early.

What other questions around $SIVE should we dig into next?

text:
9. What are the biggest risks?

The risks are real: CPO adoption can take longer, volumes can slip, dilution exists, execution must be proven.

But the multi-market setup gives downside protection. If AI photonics takes longer, wireless, SATCOM, LiDAR and defense can still drive growth.

text:
6. Is this only an AI photonics story?

No, and that is exactly what many people miss.

AI photonics is the headline, but there are several other shots on goal. The same strong III-V platform and team is now showing up across AI, LiDAR, SATCOM, telecom and defense.

Personally, the F100 consumer electronics/biosensing angle is still the joker for me. I’ve been waiting on that one for years. If that lands properly, I genuinely believe it could take us to a 2.5B+ market cap overnight. Long-cycle stuff, yes, but qualification progress still seems to be moving forward.

text:
4. Why is the WIN Semiconductor partnership so important?

Because scaling photonics is not just about technology. It’s about manufacturing.

$SIVE chose an asset-light model. Glasgow for pilot production and control. WIN Semiconductor as the high-volume foundry path.

To me, WIN is the cost-down and scaling engine behind the entire photonics story. This is what separates the companies that can actually deliver at volume from those that stay small.

$AAOI 

According to the Management, target of... almost $500 MM in Revenue per month from mid-2027 on 😱🔥🔥

Clearly supply-demand situation in Optics/Photonics Networking is so ultra tight, the only question to get to that target is.... can they execute?

$AAOI

They confirmed that the prior $371/m projection for mid-2027 is REVENUE, not capacity 

AND

They raised that projection to $471/m for mid-2027 (not including CATV)

To put it in context, they just did ~$150m in QUARTERLY revenue

text:
Top 4 photonics + asymmetric 10 bagger setups for the next decade:

$SIVE - Sivers Semiconductors
Building the optical backbone for AI datacenters. Tiny market cap with exposure to co-packaged optics, laser arrays, and next-gen AI interconnects. One of the more underfollowed photonics plays in Europe.

$LWLG - Lightwave Logic
Electro-optic polymers that could drastically reduce power consumption and latency in AI networking. If polymers become part of future silicon photonics stacks, upside is massive.

$ALMU - Aeluma
Compound semiconductors + photonics on silicon. Positioned at the intersection of sensing, quantum, AI, and defense. Very early but huge optionality.

$WOLF - Wolfspeed
Silicon carbide infrastructure for the electrification and AI power era. High risk balance sheet, but if execution improves the rerating potential is enormous.

Wildcard asymmetric names:

$MRAM - Everspin Technologies
Persistent memory for AI, aerospace, industrial, and defense systems. Small niche today but strong long-term asymmetric potential.

$ALRIB - Riber
Advanced epitaxy systems critical for compound semiconductors, photonics, and future semiconductor infrastructure.

$LPK - LPKF Laser & Electronics
Precision laser manufacturing and advanced semiconductor packaging exposure. Strong positioning if photonics and advanced chip packaging accelerate.

$SVCO - Silvaco
EDA/software infrastructure play for semiconductor design, power electronics, and emerging photonics workflows. Underfollowed picks-and-shovels angle on the semiconductor cycle.

Invest in the infrastructure that AI depends on:

$SNDK, $MU — AI Memory & Storage Infrastructure

$BE — Power Generation

$CRWV — AI Cloud Infrastructure

$INTC — Advanced Semiconductors & Foundry

$CORZ — Data Centers & HPC Hosting

$APLD — Digital Infrastructure & GPU Hosting

The applications will capture the headlines.

The infrastructure will capture the biggest returns.

text:
Goldman Sachs called optical networking a $154B market. Most of the coverage focused on transceivers and CPO vendors.

Here is how I have built exposure across six nodes in the stack, each priced differently by the market:

$SOI - Soitec
Photonics-SOI wafers. The dominant substrate layer behind silicon photonics. The market still prices it like a cyclical materials supplier.

$STMPA - STMicroelectronics
PIC chips co-developed with AWS, manufactured on 300mm wafers. Datacenter revenue guided above $500M in 2026 and above $1B in 2027. The chip layer between wafer and light source, largely absent from optical networking coverage.

$COHR - Coherent
Indium phosphide lasers. The light source inside co-packaged optics. InP is not silicon photonics. It is what makes silicon photonics work at scale. Coherent and Lumentum are the two suppliers with credible capacity.

$SIVE - Sivers Semiconductors
RF and laser components for optical transceivers. Small, Swedish.

$MRVL - Marvell Technology
Custom silicon and DSPs for high-speed optical transmission. Nvidia took an equity position. The market is starting to price this differently.

$SMOP - Smartoptics
Optical networking for edge and mid-market deployments. Q1 was misread. The $0.5M one-time cost was a 4,100 sqm expansion in Kista, just outside Stockholm, on a 7-year lease. Structural demand, not operational weakness.

Six positions across the stack.

The $154B narrative sits at the surface layer. The value chain does not.

$TEM - AI healthcare/data platform
~$50 today → potential $200 bull case

$RXRX - AI drug discovery
~$3.3 today → potential $50+ bull case
(if AI-designed therapeutics work at scale)

$LUNR - lunar infrastructure platform
~$29 today → potential $60+ bull case
(if moon economy/deep space ramps)

$QBTS - asymmetric quantum speculation
~$22.5 today → potential $40–60 bull case
(if quantum enters mainstream enterprise use)

Litografiya və ultra dəqiq optik sistemlər.

text:
Nvidia just poured $4 billion into photonics companies. That's not a portfolio diversification move. It's a confession.

The photonics bet tells you Nvidia thinks the next bottleneck for AI scaling isn't compute. It's data movement.

GPU clusters scale by passing data between thousands of chips at insane speeds. Copper interconnects are hitting physical limits. The next leap requires light, optical interconnects, fiber inside the chip package, and laser-based data transmission across racks.

Whoever owns the optical layer wins the next AI capex cycle.

The names Jason Luongo flagged are the cleanest pure-plays. $LITE Lumentum makes the lasers, with a $2 billion commitment from Nvidia and a new 240,000 sq ft InP fab in North Carolina. $MRVL Marvell owns the optical DSP layer, the brains that encode and decode data through photonic links. $GLW Corning supplies the fiber and just signed a $6 billion multiyear deal with Meta for data center buildout.

$TSM TSMC is doing co-packaged optics through its COUPE platform. $AAOI Applied Optoelectronics has $124 million in 800G orders and over $200 million in 1.6T volume orders. $SOI Soitec has a 95 percent monopoly on photonics-grade silicon-on-insulator wafers.

The bull case across all six. AI capex isn't slowing. Hyperscalers are signing multi-year contracts. Pure-play photonics names are early in the adoption curve.

The bear case. Photonics is a deeply technical field with multi-year design cycles. Any execution delay or competitive threat hits valuations hard.

Asymmetric framing. Nvidia spent $4 billion of its own balance sheet because the math told them to.

Retail can't out-research Jensen Huang. Following the capex flow is the cleanest way to position behind the same trade.

Watch the photonics basket through 2027.

text:
Everyone is chasing photonics and data center construction stocks. $COHR, $MRVL, $AVGO, $STRL, $PWR have had enormous runs. But the next wave of the AI infrastructure trade is hiding in plain sight. And one company controls the entire domestic supply chain. $MLI 

The AI buildout has moved in distinct waves, and each wave has been telegraphed well before the market priced it.

Wave 1 was chips. $NVDA, $AMD, $TSM — the compute layer that everyone needed first. That trade is mature.

Wave 2 was photonics and interconnects. $COHR, $MRVL, $AVGO, $LITE — as data volumes exploded, the market figured out that moving data between chips and servers was as important as the chips themselves. That re-rating is largely done.

Wave 3 is construction and site development. $STRL, $PWR, $EME  the companies physically building the data centers. $STRL reported earnings this week and went up 50% in a single session after disclosing that over 90% of its backlog is tied to mission-critical infrastructure projects. The market is figuring this one out in real time.

Wave 4 has not been priced. It is the companies that supply the buildout.

The physical reality of an AI data center is different from what most investors picture.

Every GPU cluster needs power delivered to it. Every watt of power generates heat that has to be removed. Every cooling system, every power distribution network, every grid connection runs on copper. There is no synthetic substitute for copper's electrical and thermal conductivity at scale. It is not a component that can be designed around.

AI servers draw four to six times more power than traditional servers. Higher power density requires thicker copper busbars, more extensive wiring, and more robust cooling infrastructure. The copper intensity of a data center scales directly with its compute density and compute density is going in one direction.

The numbers are larger than most people realize.

A modern hyperscale AI data center requires 27 to 33 tonnes of copper per megawatt of installed capacity. Microsoft's Chicago data center used approximately 2,177 tonnes of copper across an 80 MW facility. Next-generation AI campuses can consume up to 50,000 tonnes of copper per site roughly three times the copper footprint of a conventional data center.

Goldman Sachs projects that AI will drive a 165% increase in data center power demand by 2030. S&P Global estimates that data center copper demand will grow from 1.1 million metric tons in 2025 to 2.5 million metric tons by 2040. Macquarie puts annual data center copper consumption at 330,000 to 420,000 tonnes by 2030. Sprott is more aggressive, projecting 1.1 million tonnes annually by 2030 close to 3% of total global supply.

These are not marginal numbers. They represent a structural re-rating of copper demand that has no historical precedent.

The supply side cannot respond.

S&P Global projects a copper supply deficit reaching 10 million metric tons by 2040. Global copper production is expected to peak in 2030 at 33 million metric tons and then decline as ore grades fall and existing mines are depleted. Opening a new copper mine takes 20 to 30 years in developed markets. The U.S. average is closer to 29 years. UBS forecasts a refined copper deficit of more than 400,000 tonnes in 2026 alone as mine disruptions in Chile, Peru, and Indonesia collide with accelerating demand.

The structural constraint here is time. Even with significant capital investment in new mining capacity, the industry cannot meaningfully expand supply before the AI buildout peaks. This is not a commodity cycle. It is a decade-long structural imbalance.

Hyperscalers are price-insensitive buyers and this point is underappreciated.

When Microsoft, Amazon, Google, and Meta are committing hundreds of billions of dollars to AI infrastructure, copper pricing is not a material constraint on their buildout decisions. BMO Capital Markets has noted that data center developers are among the few buyers in the world who cannot easily delay or downsize projects based on metals prices. Inelastic demand meeting a structural supply deficit is a straightforward setup for sustained price appreciation.

COMEX copper averaged $5.80 per pound in Q1 2026, up 26.8% year over year. JPMorgan projects copper reaching $12,500 per tonne in Q2 2026. UBS targets $13,000 per tonne by year-end. The U.S. imposed a 50% tariff on copper imports effective August 2025, directly benefiting domestic manufacturers while compressing the margins of foreign competitors.

One company sits at the center of all of this and almost no one is talking about it.

Mueller Industries is the only vertically integrated manufacturer of copper tube, fittings, brass rod, and forgings in North America. They control the entire supply chain from raw copper to finished product. No other domestic company does this. Their products are physically present in every cooling system, every power distribution network, and every HVAC installation in every data center being built across the country supplied through HVAC OEMs like Carrier and Trane who incorporate Mueller components into their systems before they reach the job site.

The company is 70 years old. It went public in 1991. It manufactures copper fittings. And for those exact reasons, no one in AI infrastructure investing is paying attention to it.

The Q1 2026 financials are exceptional by any measure.

Revenue came in at $1.193 billion, up 19.3% year over year. Operating income grew 51.4% to $312 million. Diluted EPS of $2.16 was up 55.4% and represented the best first quarter in company history. The company ended the period with $1.39 billion in cash and zero debt a current ratio of 5.4x. Management raised the quarterly dividend 40% to $0.35 per share and repurchased $75 million of stock in the quarter alone.

Note that results include a $41.4 million one-time gain on the sale of the Sherwood Valve business. Stripping that out, clean operating income was approximately $271 million still a significant beat driven by copper pricing and underlying volume growth across industrial, electrical, and commercial markets.

The valuation gap is the most straightforward part of the thesis.

Mueller trades at 17.6x trailing earnings. The U.S. Machinery industry average is 27.5x. The peer group average is 27.5x. That is a 36% discount to comparable industrials before applying any premium for AI infrastructure exposure that competitors like Vertiv, Quanta Services, and EMCOR have already received.

The math is simple. At 25x trailing EPS of $7.76, the stock is worth $194, 43% above current levels. At 25x on forward EPS estimates of $9.50 to $10.00, the range is $237 to $250. At the peer average multiple of 27.5x, the bull case approaches $275. None of these scenarios require an AI premium. They simply require the market to stop pricing Mueller like a cyclical commodity manufacturer and start pricing it like a structural industrial compounder.

The reason the re-rating hasn't happened yet is also the reason the opportunity exists.

Vertiv sells directly to Microsoft. Quanta Services builds the sites. Mueller sells copper tube to wholesalers who sell to HVAC OEMs who sell to contractors. That three-step removal makes the AI narrative difficult to pitch in a two-page equity note. Management does not tell the AI story — on earnings calls the CEO talks about operational execution and raw material management. The company does not disclose a data center revenue segment. Investors cannot quantify what they cannot measure, so they do not pay for it.

The February 2026 selloff reinforced the cyclical narrative. When Q4 2025 EPS came in at $1.39 versus the $1.67 consensus driven by a temporary copper pricing trough the stock dropped 13% despite a 40% dividend increase. The market punished MLI as a commodity cyclical, not as a structural growth business. That is precisely the kind of mispricing that creates the entry point.

The comparison to $STRL is instructive.

Sterling Infrastructure was on every AI infrastructure watchlist for months. Analysts knew it was a data center site development company. The thesis was well understood. But the stock did not move until earnings confirmed it — specifically until management disclosed that backlog was up 78% year over year, that the company had secured initial site development work for a large multi-year semiconductor fabrication campus, and that over 90% of its E-Infrastructure backlog was tied to mission-critical projects.

One earnings report. Fifty percent in a single session.

Mueller has the same setup. The thesis is clear. The fundamentals are improving. The catalyst has not arrived yet. When management discloses data center exposure explicitly, or when a major analyst publishes a note reframing the investment case around AI infrastructure copper demand, the multiple gap will compress quickly.

The risks deserve honest treatment.

Copper price volatility is the primary risk. If COMEX copper reverses materially, Mueller's margins compress and the EPS thesis weakens. Goldman Sachs has argued that copper's rally is driven by expectations rather than fundamentals and sees prices potentially capped at $10,000 to $11,000 per tonne near term. The February selloff is a live example of how fast the market punishes MLI when copper softens. The indirect customer relationship cuts both ways — it is the source of the valuation discount and also the reason a catalyst is harder to identify and time. There is no named hyperscaler contract announcement to wait for.

The AI infrastructure trade is sequential, and the market prices each layer after the prior one becomes obvious.

Chips were priced first because they were the most visible constraint. Photonics and interconnects were priced next when it became clear that moving data was as hard as computing it. Construction and site development are being priced now $STRL's move this week is the signal.

Copper is the physical substrate underneath all of it. It goes into the cooling systems, the power distribution, the grid connections, and the fiber conduits. You cannot build the AI infrastructure stack without it. And the only vertically integrated domestic manufacturer of copper products trades at a 36% discount to its industrial peers with $1.39 billion in cash, zero debt, record earnings, and a rising dividend.

The market will connect the dots.

Wave 1 was chips. $NVDA, $AMD, $TSM — the compute layer that everyone needed first. That trade is mature. Wave 2 was photonics and interconnects. $COHR, $MRVL, $AVGO, $LITE — as data volumes exploded, the market figured out that moving data between chips and servers was as important as the chips themselves. That re-rating is largely done. Wave 3 is construction and site development. $STRL, $PWR, $EME the companies physically building the data centers. $STRL reported earnings this week and went up 50% in a single session after disclosing that over 90% of its backlog is tied to mission-critical infrastructure projects. The market is figuring this one out in real time. Wave 4 has not been priced. It is the companies that supply the buildout. The physical reality of an AI data center is different from what most investors picture. Every GPU cluster needs power delivered to it. Every watt of power generates heat that has to be removed. Every cooling system, every power distribution network, every grid connection runs on copper. There is no synthetic substitute for copper's electrical and thermal conductivity at scale. It is not a component that can be designed around. AI servers draw four to six times more power than traditional servers. Higher power density requires thicker copper busbars, more extensive wiring, and more robust cooling infrastructure. The copper intensity of a data center scales directly with its compute density and compute density is going in one direction. The numbers are larger than most people realize. A modern hyperscale AI data center requires 27 to 33 tonnes of copper per megawatt of installed capacity. Microsoft's Chicago data center used approximately 2,177 tonnes of copper across an 80 MW facility. Next-generation AI campuses can consume up to 50,000 tonnes of copper per site roughly three times the copper footprint of a conventional data center. Goldman Sachs projects that AI will drive a 165% increase in data center power demand by 2030. S&P Global estimates that data center copper demand will grow from 1.1 million metric tons in 2025 to 2.5 million metric tons by 2040. Macquarie puts annual data center copper consumption at 330,000 to 420,000 tonnes by 2030. Sprott is more aggressive, projecting 1.1 million tonnes annually by 2030 close to 3% of total global supply. These are not marginal numbers. They represent a structural re-rating of copper demand that has no historical precedent. The supply side cannot respond. S&P Global projects a copper supply deficit reaching 10 million metric tons by 2040. Global copper production is expected to peak in 2030 at 33 million metric tons and then decline as ore grades fall and existing mines are depleted. Opening a new copper mine takes 20 to 30 years in developed markets. The U.S. average is closer to 29 years. UBS forecasts a refined copper deficit of more than 400,000 tonnes in 2026 alone as mine disruptions in Chile, Peru, and Indonesia collide with accelerating demand. The structural constraint here is time. Even with significant capital investment in new mining capacity, the industry cannot meaningfully expand supply before the AI buildout peaks. This is not a commodity cycle. It is a decade-long structural imbalance. Hyperscalers are price-insensitive buyers and this point is underappreciated. When Microsoft, Amazon, Google, and Meta are committing hundreds of billions of dollars to AI infrastructure, copper pricing is not a material constraint on their buildout decisions. BMO Capital Markets has noted that data center developers are among the few buyers in the world who cannot easily delay or downsize projects based on metals prices. Inelastic demand meeting a structural supply deficit is a straightforward setup for sustained price appreciation. COMEX copper averaged $5.80 per pound in Q1 2026, up 26.8% year over year. JPMorgan projects copper reaching $12,500 per tonne in Q2 2026. UBS targets $13,000 per tonne by year-end. The U.S. imposed a 50% tariff on copper imports effective August 2025, directly benefiting domestic manufacturers while compressing the margins of foreign competitors. One company sits at the center of all of this and almost no one is talking about it. Mueller Industries is the only vertically integrated manufacturer of copper tube, fittings, brass rod, and forgings in North America. They control the entire supply chain from raw copper to finished product. No other domestic company does this. Their products are physically present in every cooling system, every power distribution network, and every HVAC installation in every data center being built across the country supplied through HVAC OEMs like Carrier and Trane who incorporate Mueller components into their systems before they reach the job site. The company is 70 years old. It went public in 1991. It manufactures copper fittings. And for those exact reasons, no one in AI infrastructure investing is paying attention to it. The Q1 2026 financials are exceptional by any measure. Revenue came in at $1.193 billion, up 19.3% year over year. Operating income grew 51.4% to $312 million. Diluted EPS of $2.16 was up 55.4% and represented the best first quarter in company history. The company ended the period with $1.39 billion in cash and zero debt a current ratio of 5.4x. Management raised the quarterly dividend 40% to $0.35 per share and repurchased $75 million of stock in the quarter alone. Note that results include a $41.4 million one-time gain on the sale of the Sherwood Valve business. Stripping that out, clean operating income was approximately $271 million still a significant beat driven by copper pricing and underlying volume growth across industrial, electrical, and commercial markets. The valuation gap is the most straightforward part of the thesis. Mueller trades at 17.6x trailing earnings. The U.S. Machinery industry average is 27.5x. The peer group average is 27.5x. That is a 36% discount to comparable industrials before applying any premium for AI infrastructure exposure that competitors like Vertiv, Quanta Services, and EMCOR have already received. The math is simple. At 25x trailing EPS of $7.76, the stock is worth $194, 43% above current levels. At 25x on forward EPS estimates of $9.50 to $10.00, the range is $237 to $250. At the peer average multiple of 27.5x, the bull case approaches $275. None of these scenarios require an AI premium. They simply require the market to stop pricing Mueller like a cyclical commodity manufacturer and start pricing it like a structural industrial compounder. The reason the re-rating hasn't happened yet is also the reason the opportunity exists. Vertiv sells directly to Microsoft. Quanta Services builds the sites. Mueller sells copper tube to wholesalers who sell to HVAC OEMs who sell to contractors. That three-step removal makes the AI narrative difficult to pitch in a two-page equity note. Management does not tell the AI story — on earnings calls the CEO talks about operational execution and raw material management. The company does not disclose a data center revenue segment. Investors cannot quantify what they cannot measure, so they do not pay for it. The February 2026 selloff reinforced the cyclical narrative. When Q4 2025 EPS came in at $1.39 versus the $1.67 consensus driven by a temporary copper pricing trough the stock dropped 13% despite a 40% dividend increase. The market punished MLI as a commodity cyclical, not as a structural growth business. That is precisely the kind of mispricing that creates the entry point. The comparison to $STRL is instructive. Sterling Infrastructure was on every AI infrastructure watchlist for months. Analysts knew it was a data center site development company. The thesis was well understood. But the stock did not move until earnings confirmed it — specifically until management disclosed that backlog was up 78% year over year, that the company had secured initial site development work for a large multi-year semiconductor fabrication campus, and that over 90% of its E-Infrastructure backlog was tied to mission-critical projects. One earnings report. Fifty percent in a single session. Mueller has the same setup. The thesis is clear. The fundamentals are improving. The catalyst has not arrived yet. When management discloses data center exposure explicitly, or when a major analyst publishes a note reframing the investment case around AI infrastructure copper demand, the multiple gap will compress quickly. The risks deserve honest treatment. Copper price volatility is the primary risk. If COMEX copper reverses materially, Mueller's margins compress and the EPS thesis weakens. Goldman Sachs has argued that copper's rally is driven by expectations rather than fundamentals and sees prices potentially capped at $10,000 to $11,000 per tonne near term. The February selloff is a live example of how fast the market punishes MLI when copper softens. The indirect customer relationship cuts both ways — it is the source of the valuation discount and also the reason a catalyst is harder to identify and time. There is no named hyperscaler contract announcement to wait for. The AI infrastructure trade is sequential, and the market prices each layer after the prior one becomes obvious. Chips were priced first because they were the most visible constraint. Photonics and interconnects were priced next when it became clear that moving data was as hard as computing it. Construction and site development are being priced now $STRL's move this week is the signal. Copper is the physical substrate underneath all of it. It goes into the cooling systems, the power distribution, the grid connections, and the fiber conduits. You cannot build the AI infrastructure stack without it. And the only vertically integrated domestic manufacturer of copper products trades at a 36% discount to its industrial peers with $1.39 billion in cash, zero debt, record earnings, and a rising dividend. The market will connect the dots."}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 0, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2051775198110810417", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "216", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
"A diversified portfolio in 2026: 25% neoclouds, 25% CPU plays, 25% international photonics, 25% memory."

This is the cleanest tongue-in-cheek summary of where the AI capex cycle has actually concentrated.

Neoclouds. CoreWeave, Nebius, Lambda. Pure-play GPU infrastructure providers riding the AI compute wave. CoreWeave alone has signed multi-billion dollar contracts with Microsoft and OpenAI. The category didn't exist as a public investable theme three years ago.

CPU plays. Intel ($INTC), AMD, Arm Holdings. The forgotten layer that came roaring back as AI workloads shifted from pure GPU to mixed CPU-GPU architectures. AMD's data center revenue is up triple digits year over year.

International photonics. ASML for EUV lithography, Lumentum and Coherent for optical interconnect, TSMC for foundry. The "photonics" basket is increasingly the entire physical infrastructure that makes silicon work at AI scale.

Memory. Micron ($MU), Samsung, SK Hynix. High-bandwidth memory is the actual bottleneck for AI training, and memory makers have pricing power that hasn't been seen since the 2017-18 cycle.

What this portfolio tells you. Diversification used to mean different sectors. In 2026, "diversified" means different layers of the same AI infrastructure stack.

That's a real concentration risk hiding behind apparent variety.

If AI capex turns or if hyperscaler spending pauses, all four buckets correlate hard to the downside.

The honest read. This portfolio is a bet that AI infrastructure spending continues unabated through 2028 and beyond.

If that thesis holds, it prints. If it doesn't, all four legs of the table fold at the same time.

Diversification across one trade is concentration with extra steps.

Size accordingly.

text:
Who Wins the Photonics Stack: A Tier-by-Tier Financial Classification of Every Company

Knowing the players in the Silicon Photonics supply chain is one thing—knowing which ones are structurally and financially built to dominate is another.

In my latest deep dive, I’ve classified every public company across all 8 layers of the stack into three distinct financial tiers. We move beyond the "science projects" to identify the actual bedrock of the AI optical backbone.

The Classification Matrix:

Tier-1: The Bedrock. Proven revenue, dominant market share, and irreplaceable moat (e.g., $ASML, $LITE, $TSM, $FN).

Tier-2: The Scale-ups. Clear growth thesis, but still consolidating market position (e.g., $SOI, $IQE, $CAMT).

Moonshots: High-asymmetry bets. Zero/minimal revenue today, but own the tech that could disrupt everything (e.g., $AXTI, $LWLG, $HIMX).

Key Architectural Insights:

Vertical Integration: Why $COHR is currently the only benchmark spanning 6 layers.

The Foundry Shift: How $TSM’s entry is re-rating the entire Layer 2 landscape.

Supply Bottlenecks: Identifying the companies that own the "chokepoint" materials like InP.

Personally, I see this tiering as the essential roadmap for anyone serious about AI infrastructure. The "AI trade" is shifting from the chip to the fabric, and these are the tiers that matter.

Read the full financial map here: https://t.co/auOeAcLwkl 

My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). Full disclosure in my pinned tweet.

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 497}, {"from_index": 865, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 892}]}, "text": "Who Wins the Photonics Stack: A Tier-by-Tier Financial Classification of Every Company Knowing the players in the Silicon Photonics supply chain is one thing—knowing which ones are structurally and financially built to dominate is another. In my latest deep dive, I’ve classified every public company across all 8 layers of the stack into three distinct financial tiers. We move beyond the "science projects" to identify the actual bedrock of the AI optical backbone. The Classification Matrix: Tier-1: The Bedrock. Proven revenue, dominant market share, and irreplaceable moat (e.g., $ASML, $LITE, $TSM, $FN). Tier-2: The Scale-ups. Clear growth thesis, but still consolidating market position (e.g., $SOI, $IQE, $CAMT). Moonshots: High-asymmetry bets. Zero/minimal revenue today, but own the tech that could disrupt everything (e.g., $AXTI, $LWLG, $HIMX). Key Architectural Insights: Vertical Integration: Why $COHR is currently the only benchmark spanning 6 layers. The Foundry Shift: How $TSM’s entry is re-rating the entire Layer 2 landscape. Supply Bottlenecks: Identifying the companies that own the "chokepoint" materials like InP. Personally, I see this tiering as the essential roadmap for anyone serious about AI infrastructure. The "AI trade" is shifting from the chip to the fabric, and these are the tiers that matter. Read the full financial map here: https://t.co/auOeAcLwkl My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). text:
The Photonics Stack: A Layer-by-Layer Guide to Every Company Building the Optical Backbone of AI
The Analytical Edge Strategy
May 11, 2026

A complete map of the silicon photonics supply chain — from raw indium to fiber in the ground

If you’ve been following AI infrastructure, you’ve probably heard that data centers are hitting what some call the Copper Wall — the point where electrical signals through copper can no longer keep up with the heat, latency, and bandwidth demands of training and running massive AI models. The answer is photonics: moving data as light through fiber instead of electrons through metal.

But photonics isn’t a single product. It’s a deep, layered supply chain stretching from exotic crystal growth in China to fiber trenching across American highways. This guide maps every layer of that chain, names every major public company in it, and explains exactly what each one does.

No data has been omitted. Every company from the original research is here.

Layer 0 — Raw Materials
What this layer means: This is the floor of the photonics supply chain. Before any chip can be designed, any laser built, or any fiber laid, the raw materials must exist. Indium phosphide (InP), silicon-on-insulator (SOI), gallium arsenide (GaAs), and optical-grade glass fiber are the fundamental outputs of this layer. Every laser, chip, and cable in the chain depends on what gets produced here. It is also the most geopolitically exposed layer in the entire stack: approximately 60–70% of global indium production comes from China.

$AXTI — AXT Inc. Single-crystal InP and GaAs wafer manufacturer. These substrates are the irreplaceable foundation for EML lasers and high-speed photodiodes. NVIDIA’s aggressive push toward 1.6T transceivers has turned InP into a supply bottleneck, and AXTI sits directly at that chokepoint.

$IQE III-V compound semiconductor epitaxy specialist. IQE grows thin-film layers of InP, GaAs, and GaN onto customer wafers, supplying the epitaxial substrate that laser diode and high-speed photodetector manufacturers need. Often called the invisible backbone of the optical module supply chain.

$SOI — Soitec Silicon-on-Insulator wafer manufacturer. SOI wafers are the starting material for silicon photonics foundries. TSMC’s COUPE platform, GlobalFoundries’ Fotonix process, and Tower Semiconductor’s SiPh line all rely on Soitec substrates. When a Broadcom or Marvell photonic chip ships inside a data center, there is a good chance the journey started on a Soitec wafer.

$WOLF — Wolfspeed Silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN) wafer manufacturer. Not directly tied to photonic lasers, but critical for the high-power electronics and power converters inside data center infrastructure. Wolfspeed feeds the power layer that photonics depends on to function.

$GLW — Corning (also active in Layer 5) The world’s largest manufacturer of optical fiber and specialty glass. Every fiber cable running inside and between data centers starts here. Corning also produces specialty glass components used in transceiver housings and is a named partner in NVIDIA’s Spectrum-X silicon photonics ecosystem. Active in both Layer 0 (raw glass and fiber) and Layer 5 (module components).

$LWLG — Lightwave Logic Polymer-based electro-optic waveguide developer. Rather than silicon, LWLG uses organic polymer materials in the modulators that convert electrical signals to light — promising significantly higher modulation speeds and lower insertion loss. Still in the commercialization phase, making this an early-stage bet on a materials platform that could disrupt the conventional silicon approach.

$SHMN — Shin-Etsu Chemical (also active in Equipment layer) The world’s largest silicon wafer manufacturer. While SiPh foundries use SOI specifically, Shin-Etsu supplies the standard silicon wafer base that underpins the entire semiconductor ecosystem. Also present in the equipment layer as a foundry supply chain essential.

$VECO — Veeco Instruments (also active in Equipment layer) Manufacturer of MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy) and MOCVD (Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition) equipment. Veeco does not make wafers — it makes the machines that grow them. III-V laser wafer production is impossible without MOCVD reactors, which is where Veeco’s role sits. Active in both Layer 0 (enabling raw material production) and the Equipment layer.

End of Layer 0.
There are 6 layer more.

So you can read my full report in my substack as free;
https://t.co/CZIKj5KHVp

My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). Full disclosure in my pinned tweet.

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 1011}]}, "text": "The Photonics Stack: A Layer-by-Layer Guide to Every Company Building the Optical Backbone of AI The Analytical Edge Strategy May 11, 2026 A complete map of the silicon photonics supply chain — from raw indium to fiber in the ground If you’ve been following AI infrastructure, you’ve probably heard that data centers are hitting what some call the Copper Wall — the point where electrical signals through copper can no longer keep up with the heat, latency, and bandwidth demands of training and running massive AI models. The answer is photonics: moving data as light through fiber instead of electrons through metal. But photonics isn’t a single product. It’s a deep, layered supply chain stretching from exotic crystal growth in China to fiber trenching across American highways. This guide maps every layer of that chain, names every major public company in it, and explains exactly what each one does. No data has been omitted. Every company from the original research is here. Layer 0 — Raw Materials What this layer means: This is the floor of the photonics supply chain. Before any chip can be designed, any laser built, or any fiber laid, the raw materials must exist. Indium phosphide (InP), silicon-on-insulator (SOI), gallium arsenide (GaAs), and optical-grade glass fiber are the fundamental outputs of this layer. Every laser, chip, and cable in the chain depends on what gets produced here. It is also the most geopolitically exposed layer in the entire stack: approximately 60–70% of global indium production comes from China. $AXTI — AXT Inc. Single-crystal InP and GaAs wafer manufacturer. These substrates are the irreplaceable foundation for EML lasers and high-speed photodiodes. NVIDIA’s aggressive push toward 1.6T transceivers has turned InP into a supply bottleneck, and AXTI sits directly at that chokepoint. $IQE III-V compound semiconductor epitaxy specialist. IQE grows thin-film layers of InP, GaAs, and GaN onto customer wafers, supplying the epitaxial substrate that laser diode and high-speed photodetector manufacturers need. Often called the invisible backbone of the optical module supply chain. $SOI — Soitec Silicon-on-Insulator wafer manufacturer. SOI wafers are the starting material for silicon photonics foundries. TSMC’s COUPE platform, GlobalFoundries’ Fotonix process, and Tower Semiconductor’s SiPh line all rely on Soitec substrates. When a Broadcom or Marvell photonic chip ships inside a data center, there is a good chance the journey started on a Soitec wafer. $WOLF — Wolfspeed Silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN) wafer manufacturer. Not directly tied to photonic lasers, but critical for the high-power electronics and power converters inside data center infrastructure. Wolfspeed feeds the power layer that photonics depends on to function. $GLW — Corning (also active in Layer 5) The world’s largest manufacturer of optical fiber and specialty glass. Every fiber cable running inside and between data centers starts here. Corning also produces specialty glass components used in transceiver housings and is a named partner in NVIDIA’s Spectrum-X silicon photonics ecosystem. Active in both Layer 0 (raw glass and fiber) and Layer 5 (module components). $LWLG — Lightwave Logic Polymer-based electro-optic waveguide developer. Rather than silicon, LWLG uses organic polymer materials in the modulators that convert electrical signals to light — promising significantly higher modulation speeds and lower insertion loss. Still in the commercialization phase, making this an early-stage bet on a materials platform that could disrupt the conventional silicon approach. $SHMN — Shin-Etsu Chemical (also active in Equipment layer) The world’s largest silicon wafer manufacturer. While SiPh foundries use SOI specifically, Shin-Etsu supplies the standard silicon wafer base that underpins the entire semiconductor ecosystem. Also present in the equipment layer as a foundry supply chain essential. $VECO — Veeco Instruments (also active in Equipment layer) Manufacturer of MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy) and MOCVD (Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition) equipment. Veeco does not make wafers — it makes the machines that grow them. III-V laser wafer production is impossible without MOCVD reactors, which is where Veeco’s role sits. Active in both Layer 0 (enabling raw material production) and the Equipment layer. End of Layer 0. There are 6 layer more. So you can read my full report in my substack as free; https://t.co/CZIKj5KHVp My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA).
